Urn model for products’ shares in international trade

Matthieu Barbier Centre for Biodiversity Theory and Modelling, Theoretical and Experimental Ecology Station, CNRS, Moulis 09200 , France    D.-S. Lee Department of Physics, Inha University, Incheon 22212, Korea deoksun.lee@inha.ac.kr
Abstract

International trade fluxes evolve as countries revise their portfolios of trade products towards economic development. Accordingly products’ shares in international trade vary with time, reflecting the transfer of capital between distinct industrial sectors. Here we analyze the share of hundreds of product categories in world trade for four decades and find a scaling law obeyed by the annual variation of product share, which informs us of how capital flows and interacts over the product space. A model of stochastic transfer of capital between products based on the observed scaling relation is proposed and shown to reproduce exactly the empirical share distribution. The model allows analytic solutions as well as numerical simulations, which predict a pseudo-condensation of capital onto few product categories and when it will occur. At the individual level, our model finds certain products unpredictable, the excess or deficient growth of which with respect to the model prediction is shown to be correlated with the nature of goods.

I Introduction

Finite and uneven distribution of resources and capabilities for production lead a huge volume of products and capital to be exchanged across countries. The organization of such international trade has long been studied to elucidate the impact of the international relations, geography and sociocultural factors on trade fluxes Tinbergen (1962), as represented by e.g., the gravity model Anderson (2010); Barigozzi et al. (2010), as well as the topology of the trade network of countries Serrano and Boguñá (2003); Garlaschelli and Loffredo (2004).

The fluctuation of trade volumes of various products also carries valuable information on human economic activities. As a country’s portfolio of exports is crucial for both its immediate success in the global market and long-term development Hidalgo et al. (2007); Hidalgo and Hausmann (2009); Hausmann and Hidalgo (2011); Tacchella et al. (2012), political and economic agents often shift their investment strategy from one product to another, which affect the market share of related products. Aggregated together, a huge number of such strategy shifts across countries, time, and products cause fluctuations of products’ shares, which can provide a clue for understanding a principle of capital management towards best benefiting from producing and exporting selected goods.

Here we report our finding of a scaling behavior of the annual variation of trade products’ shares in international trade. It is shown to imply that capital invested for various product categories, identified here with their shares, tends to move to and from popular products, the probability of which is quadratically proportional to their current shares. A stochastic model of such biquadratic transfer of discretized capital among products is proposed, which reproduces the empirical distribution and evolution of product share, confirming that the shifts of investment strategy are made mostly referring to the current shares of products. Random transfer models have been similarly proposed to explain the distribution of wealth among individuals or countries Yakovenko and Rosser Jr (2009); Bouchaud and Mézard (2000). The functional form of the transfer rate determines whether the system is either in the fluid phase or the condensate phase with the biquadratic one at the boundary. Analytic solutions and simulation results reveal the possibility of condensation of capital onto few key products and predict its time scale. Therefore the studied model can be used as a framework helping understand and analyze the overwhelming complexity of international trade.

II Distribution and annual variation of product share

We analyze the NBER-UN dataset Feenstra et al. (2005), which contains trade volumes of N=508𝑁508N=508 product types based on the Standard International Trade Classification (SITC Rev. 2) and consistently reported over the period 1962-2000. A single datum Vc,c′,p​(t)subscript𝑉𝑐superscript𝑐′𝑝𝑡V_{c,c^{\prime},p}(t) is the amount of trade (in nominal dollars) from country c𝑐c to country c′superscript𝑐′c^{\prime} in product category p𝑝p in the year t𝑡t. We investigate product share

Ap​(t)=∑c,c′Vc,c′,p​(t)∑c,c′,p′Vc,c′,p′​(t),subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡subscript𝑐superscript𝑐′subscript𝑉𝑐superscript𝑐′𝑝𝑡subscript𝑐superscript𝑐′superscript𝑝′subscript𝑉𝑐superscript𝑐′superscript𝑝′𝑡A_{p}(t)=\dfrac{\sum_{c,c^{\prime}}V_{c,c^{\prime},p}(t)}{\sum_{c,c^{\prime},p^{\prime}}V_{c,c^{\prime},p^{\prime}}(t)}, (1)

which is the fraction of the total amount of money involved in trade worldwide that is exchanged with product p𝑝p, and thus represents the popularity of this product in the global economy. While the total trade volume Vall​(t)=∑c,c′,pVc,c′,p​(t)subscript𝑉all𝑡subscript𝑐superscript𝑐′𝑝subscript𝑉𝑐superscript𝑐′𝑝𝑡V_{\rm all}(t)=\sum_{c,c^{\prime},p}V_{c,c^{\prime},p}(t) grows exponentially, we are here interested in the investment-strategy shifts among various product categories, which are entirely contained in the dynamics of Ap​(t)subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡A_{p}(t).

Remarkably a small number of products occupy a large fraction of total trade Mantegna and Stanley (1995). Crude oil (SITC 3330) is the category with the largest share, retaining close to 10%percent1010\% of all trade for the whole period. By contrast, the share for e.g. the category of slag and similar waste (SITC 2786) never exceeds 10−3superscript10310^{-3}. The general shape of the probability density function of product share Pt​(A)=∑pδ​(Ap​(t)−A)subscript𝑃𝑡𝐴subscript𝑝𝛿subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡𝐴P_{t}(A)=\sum_{p}\delta(A_{p}(t)-A) is close to a lognormal function Pt​(A)∼A−1​exp⁡[−(log⁡A−μ)22​σ2]similar-tosubscript𝑃𝑡𝐴superscript𝐴1superscript𝐴𝜇22superscript𝜎2P_{t}(A)\sim A^{-1}\exp\left[-{(\log A-\mu)^{2}\over 2\sigma^{2}}\right] as shown in Fig. 1 (a) or a stretched-exponential function Pt​(A)∼Aβ−1​exp⁡[−λ​Aβ]similar-tosubscript𝑃𝑡𝐴superscript𝐴𝛽1𝜆superscript𝐴𝛽P_{t}(A)\sim A^{\beta-1}\exp[-\lambda A^{\beta}] Clauset et al. (2009); Alstott et al. (2014).

Many products’ share exhibits significant variation over time, as in the case of orthopedic appliances (8996) and copra (2231) which display steady growth and decline respectively over orders of magnitude in their shares [Fig. 1 (b)]. We find that the annual variation of the share of an individual product category Δ​Ap​(t)=Ap​(t+1)−Ap​(t)Δsubscript𝐴𝑝𝑡subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡1subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡\Delta A_{p}(t)=A_{p}(t+1)-A_{p}(t), when averaged over gains (Δ​Ap>0Δsubscript𝐴𝑝0\Delta A_{p}>0) or losses (Δ​Ap<0Δsubscript𝐴𝑝0\Delta A_{p}<0), is found to be proportional to the current share as

|Δ​Ap​(t)|≃c​Ap​(t)similar-to-or-equalsΔsubscript𝐴𝑝𝑡𝑐subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡|\Delta A_{p}(t)|\simeq cA_{p}(t) (2)

with c≃0.1similar-to-or-equals𝑐0.1c\simeq 0.1 [Fig. 1 (c)]. For A𝐴A so small as A≲10−5less-than-or-similar-to𝐴superscript105A\lesssim 10^{-5}, the gain of product share shows fluctuations. The linear scaling in Eq. (2) appears also in other economic time series Gibrat (1931); Mantegna and Stanley (1995). Considering Ap​(t)subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡A_{p}(t) as approximating the time average ⟨Ap⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝐴𝑝\langle A_{p}\rangle of p𝑝p in the period between t𝑡t and t+1𝑡1t+1 and Δ​Ap​(t)Δsubscript𝐴𝑝𝑡\Delta A_{p}(t) as the standard deviation σpsubscript𝜎𝑝\sigma_{p} in the same period, we find Eq. (2) represent the fluctuation scaling σp∼⟨Ap⟩αsimilar-tosubscript𝜎𝑝superscriptdelimited-⟨⟩subscript𝐴𝑝𝛼\sigma_{p}\sim\langle A_{p}\rangle^{\alpha} with α=1𝛼1\alpha=1, which has been investigated for diverse complex systems de Menezes and Barabási (2004); Eisler et al. (2008); Kendal and Jørgensen (2011) under such names as Gibrat’s law Gibrat (1931) or Taylor’s law Taylor (1961). Though simple, Eq. (2) has far-reaching implications for the underlying dynamics of capital invested for product categories, which we address in the next sections.

III Urn model

Differentiated evolution of individual product share arise from a huge number of microscopic changes of investment made by companies and countries, which can be modeled as independent trajectories subject to non-gaussian stochasticity Mantegna and Stanley (1995) or as emerging from exchange between individuals Yakovenko and Rosser Jr (2009); Bouchaud and Mézard (2000). We take the latter approach to understand the origin of the empirical features of product share presented in Sec. II.

As the first step of modeling the kinetics of product share, we discretize the product share by introducing a unit share a𝑎a, representing the amount available for a single strategic change. Then we find the continuous share Apsubscript𝐴𝑝A_{p} discretized to mp=Ap/asubscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝐴𝑝𝑎m_{p}=A_{p}/a particles, resulting in a total of M=∑p=1Nmp=1/a𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑝1𝑁subscript𝑚𝑝1𝑎M=\sum_{p=1}^{N}m_{p}=1/a particles distributed over N𝑁N urns or sites (products). Transferring a particle from a site to another represents the flow of capital between them caused by the change of investment strategy of microscopic agents on a short time scale. We assume that those share particles hop from a site to another stochastically and independently. Such a particle-hopping model can be classified as the urn model Godreche and Luck (2002); Evans and Hanney (2005) in which a site p𝑝p sends one of its particles to another site q𝑞q with rate up​qsubscript𝑢𝑝𝑞u_{pq} varying with both the departure and destination site [Fig. 2 (a)].

III.1 Biquadratic transfer rate

The transfer rate up​qsubscript𝑢𝑝𝑞u_{pq} can be determined to satisfy Eq. (2). A sequence of 𝒩𝒩\mathcal{N} particle jumps in or out of a site p𝑝p sums up to increase or decrease the number of particles at p𝑝p by |Δ​mp|∼𝒩similar-toΔsubscript𝑚𝑝𝒩|\Delta m_{p}|\sim\sqrt{\mathcal{N}} as in random walks Hughes (1995). Therefore the linear scaling in Eq. (2) can be reproduced if a site p𝑝p is selected 𝒩∝mp2proportional-to𝒩superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝2\mathcal{N}\propto m_{p}^{2} times per unit time. This reasoning leads us to the transfer rate up​qsubscript𝑢𝑝𝑞u_{pq} being biquadratic in the numbers of particles at p𝑝p and q𝑞q. That is, b=2𝑏2b=2 in

up​q=u​(mp,mq)=(mp​mq)b∑ℓmℓb.subscript𝑢𝑝𝑞𝑢subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑞superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑞𝑏subscriptℓsuperscriptsubscript𝑚ℓ𝑏u_{pq}=u(m_{p},m_{q})=\dfrac{(m_{p}m_{q})^{b}}{\sum_{\ell}m_{\ell}^{b}}. (3)

If b=0𝑏0b=0, sites are uniformly selected, the number of particles at a site follows the Boltzmann distribution in the stationary state p∞​(m)=ρ−1​e−m/ρsubscript𝑝𝑚superscript𝜌1superscript𝑒𝑚𝜌p_{\infty}(m)=\rho^{-1}e^{-m/\rho} with the particle density ρ≡M/N𝜌𝑀𝑁\rho\equiv M/N Yakovenko and Rosser Jr (2009). If b≠0𝑏0b\neq 0, sites are given unequal chances for particle hopping. If we consider particle hopping as occurring due to attraction between particles and assume that up​qsubscript𝑢𝑝𝑞u_{pq} is proportional to the sum of those attractions between all pairs of particles at site p𝑝p and q𝑞q, we find that individual particles attract one another with equal strength for b=1𝑏1b=1. The quadratic scaling b=2𝑏2b=2, reproducing the linear scaling in Eq. (2), implies inhomogeneous interaction between particles; the interaction between a particle at site p𝑝p and another at q𝑞q is proportional to mp​mqsubscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑞m_{p}m_{q}. It should be mentioned that a site having only one particle is not allowed to send a particle elsewhere, i.e., up​q=0subscript𝑢𝑝𝑞0u_{pq}=0 if mp=1subscript𝑚𝑝1m_{p}=1, since our study is restricted to the products maintaining non-zero share in the studied period.

III.2 Stationary-state distribution and phase diagram

To see how particles are distributed under Eq. (3), let us consider the evolution of the probability of finding a particle configuration {m}={mp|p=1,2,…,N}𝑚conditional-setsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑝12…𝑁\{m\}=\{m_{p}|p=1,2,\ldots,N\} with time t~~𝑡{\tilde{t}} Evans and Hanney (2005):

∂pt~​({m})∂t~=∑p<q[u(mp+1,mq−1)pt~({m}p​q(+−))\displaystyle{\partial p_{{\tilde{t}}}(\{m\})\over\partial{\tilde{t}}}=\sum_{p<q}\left[u(m_{p}+1,m_{q}-1)\,p_{\tilde{t}}(\{m\}_{pq}^{(+-)})\right.
−u​(mq,mp)​pt~​({m})+u​(mq+1,mp−1)​pt~​({m}p​q(−+))𝑢subscript𝑚𝑞subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑝~𝑡𝑚𝑢subscript𝑚𝑞1subscript𝑚𝑝1subscript𝑝~𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑞absent\displaystyle-u(m_{q},m_{p})\,p_{\tilde{t}}(\{m\})+u(m_{q}+1,m_{p}-1)\,p_{\tilde{t}}(\{m\}_{pq}^{(-+)})
−u(mp,mq)pt~({m})],\displaystyle\left.-u(m_{p},m_{q})\,p_{\tilde{t}}(\{m\})\right], (4)

where {m}p​q(±,∓)superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑞plus-or-minusminus-or-plus\{m\}_{pq}^{(\pm,\mp)} is identical to {m}𝑚\{m\} except at sites p𝑝p and q𝑞q such that {m}p​q(±,∓)={m1,m2,…,mp−1,mp±1,mp+1,…,mq−1,mq∓1,mq+1,…}superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑞plus-or-minusminus-or-plussubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2…subscript𝑚𝑝1plus-or-minussubscript𝑚𝑝1subscript𝑚𝑝1…subscript𝑚𝑞1minus-or-plussubscript𝑚𝑞1subscript𝑚𝑞1…\{m\}_{pq}^{(\pm,\mp)}=\{m_{1},m_{2},\ldots,m_{p-1},m_{p}\pm 1,m_{p+1},\ldots,m_{q-1},m_{q}\mp 1,m_{q+1},\ldots\}. In the stationary state (t~→∞)→~𝑡({\tilde{t}}\to\infty), the detailed balance condition u​(mp+1,mq−1)​p∞​({m}p​q(+−))=u​(mq,mp)​p∞​({m})𝑢subscript𝑚𝑝1subscript𝑚𝑞1subscript𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑞absent𝑢subscript𝑚𝑞subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑝𝑚u(m_{p}+1,m_{q}-1)\,p_{\infty}(\{m\}_{pq}^{(+-)})=u(m_{q},m_{p})\,p_{\infty}(\{m\}) (and equivalently u​(mq+1,mp−1)​p∞​({m}p​q(−+))=u​(mp,mq)​p∞​({m})𝑢subscript𝑚𝑞1subscript𝑚𝑝1subscript𝑝superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑞absent𝑢subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑞subscript𝑝𝑚u(m_{q}+1,m_{p}-1)\,p_{\infty}(\{m\}_{pq}^{(-+)})=u(m_{p},m_{q})\,p_{\infty}(\{m\})) is satisfied if u​(mp,mq)𝑢subscript𝑚𝑝subscript𝑚𝑞u(m_{p},m_{q}) is the multiplication of a function of mpsubscript𝑚𝑝m_{p} and a function of mqsubscript𝑚𝑞m_{q} Evans and Hanney (2005), which is approximately true in the stationary state of our model; the denominator of Eq. (3) saturates in the long time limit as confirmed in Fig. 3 (b). Inserting Eq. (3) in the detailed-balance condition, one finds that p∞​({m})subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(\{m\}) takes a factorized form

p∞​({m})subscript𝑝𝑚\displaystyle p_{\infty}(\{m\}) =1ZN,M​∏i=1Nfb​(mi),absent1subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑖1𝑁subscript𝑓𝑏subscript𝑚𝑖\displaystyle={1\over Z_{N,M}}\prod_{i=1}^{N}f_{b}(m_{i}),
fb​(m)subscript𝑓𝑏𝑚\displaystyle f_{b}(m) =1ζ​(b)​mb,absent1𝜁𝑏superscript𝑚𝑏\displaystyle={1\over\zeta(b)m^{b}},
ZN,Msubscript𝑍𝑁𝑀\displaystyle Z_{N,M} =∏i=1N(∑mi=1∞fb​(mi))​δ​(∑j=1Nmj−M),absentsuperscriptsubscriptproduct𝑖1𝑁superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚𝑖1subscript𝑓𝑏subscript𝑚𝑖𝛿superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝑁subscript𝑚𝑗𝑀\displaystyle=\prod_{i=1}^{N}\left(\sum_{m_{i}=1}^{\infty}f_{b}(m_{i})\right)\delta\left(\sum_{j=1}^{N}m_{j}-M\right), (5)

with ζ​(x)𝜁𝑥\zeta(x) the Riemann zeta function and ZN,Msubscript𝑍𝑁𝑀Z_{N,M} the partition function. While many properties of the factorized states in Eq. (5) have been investigated Burda et al. (2002); Majumdar et al. (2005); Evans et al. (2006), we have seen that the case of b=2𝑏2b=2 is the model for the kinetics of product share in international trade, the properties of which are not fully understood.

Of main interest is the particle-number distribution at a single site, which is obtained from the whole particle configuration probability p∞​({m})subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(\{m\}) as p∞​(m)=∑{m2,m2,…,}p∞​({m,m2,m3,…})p_{\infty}(m)=\sum_{\{m_{2},m_{2},\ldots,\}}p_{\infty}(\{m,m_{2},m_{3},\ldots\}) by using Eq. (5) Evans and Hanney (2005)

p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚\displaystyle p_{\infty}(m) =fb​(m)​ZN−1,M−mZN,M=1ζ​(b)​mb​ZN−1,M−mZN,M.absentsubscript𝑓𝑏𝑚subscript𝑍𝑁1𝑀𝑚subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀1𝜁𝑏superscript𝑚𝑏subscript𝑍𝑁1𝑀𝑚subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀\displaystyle=f_{b}(m){Z_{N-1,M-m}\over Z_{N,M}}={1\over\zeta(b)m^{b}}{Z_{N-1,M-m}\over Z_{N,M}}. (6)

The particle-number distribution p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) behaves as p∞​(m)≃fb​(m)=m−b/ζ​(b)similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑝𝑚subscript𝑓𝑏𝑚superscript𝑚𝑏𝜁𝑏p_{\infty}(m)\simeq f_{b}(m)=m^{-b}/\zeta(b) for m𝑚m small while the contribution of ZN−1,M−m/ZN,Msubscript𝑍𝑁1𝑀𝑚subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀Z_{N-1,M-m}/Z_{N,M} in Eq. (6) may not be negligible for m𝑚m large. When the particle density ρ𝜌\rho is small, the latter contribution takes the form e−μ​msuperscript𝑒𝜇𝑚e^{-\mu\,m} with μ𝜇\mu depending on ρ𝜌\rho via the relation ∑m=1∞m​p∞​(m)=ρsuperscriptsubscript𝑚1𝑚subscript𝑝𝑚𝜌\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}m\,p_{\infty}(m)=\rho. μ𝜇\mu corresponds to the negative chemical potential μ=(∂/∂M)​log⁡ZN,M𝜇𝑀subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀\mu=(\partial/\partial M)\log Z_{N,M} Evans et al. (2006), and decreases with increasing ρ𝜌\rho. For b>2𝑏2b>2, there exists the critical density ρcsubscript𝜌𝑐\rho_{c} such that the relation ∑m=1∞m​p∞​(m)=ρsuperscriptsubscript𝑚1𝑚subscript𝑝𝑚𝜌\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}m\,p_{\infty}(m)=\rho can be satisfied by p∞​(m)=fb​(m)​e−μ​msubscript𝑝𝑚subscript𝑓𝑏𝑚superscript𝑒𝜇𝑚p_{\infty}(m)=f_{b}(m)e^{-\mu\,m} with μ≥0𝜇0\mu\geq 0 only for ρ≤ρc𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐\rho\leq\rho_{c}:

ρc=ζ​(b−1)ζ​(b).subscript𝜌𝑐𝜁𝑏1𝜁𝑏\rho_{c}={\zeta(b-1)\over\zeta(b)}. (7)

If ρ>ρc𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐\rho>\rho_{c}, p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) develops additionally a bump in the region m≃M−N​ρcsimilar-to-or-equals𝑚𝑀𝑁subscript𝜌𝑐m\simeq M-N\rho_{c} indicating that most particles occupy a single site, which may be called condensation Evans et al. (2006). For b≤2𝑏2b\leq 2 and ρ𝜌\rho finite, p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) is free from such a bump in the limit N→∞→𝑁N\to\infty. Therefore two phases, fluid and condensate phase, can be defined depending on whether such condensation occurs or not in the stationary state, which leads to the phase diagram in the (b,ρ)𝑏𝜌(b,\rho) plane [Fig. 2 (b)].

IV Urn model with b=2𝑏2b=2 for product share kinetics

The phase diagram in Fig. 2 (b) indicates that b=2𝑏2b=2 is a critical value: Condensate emerges for finite particle density only when b𝑏b is larger than 222. Given that the kinetics of product share in international trade is described by the model with b=2𝑏2b=2, one can expect that there will be no condensation in international trade. However in reality N𝑁N is large but finite and then a bump may appear in p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) even with b≤2𝑏2b\leq 2 for ρ𝜌\rho sufficiently large, which is called pseudo-condensate Evans et al. (2006). In this section, we bring the urn model with b=2𝑏2b=2 as close as possible to the real trade by fitting the model parameters, mainly the particle density ρ𝜌\rho and the time scale, to address the model’s power of explaining the international trade in the past and predicting its future.

IV.1 Time scale corresponding to one year

Let Δ​t~Δ~𝑡\Delta\tilde{t} denote the time interval in the model corresponding to one year in the real world. For the time interval Δ​t~Δ~𝑡\Delta\tilde{t}, a site p𝑝p is selected 2​mp2​Δ​t~2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑝2Δ~𝑡2m_{p}^{2}\Delta\tilde{t} times for gaining or losing a particle with the rate in Eq. (3) and b=2𝑏2b=2, which either increases or decreases mpsubscript𝑚𝑝m_{p} by Hughes (1995)

|⟨Δ​mp⟩|≃(2/π)​𝒩≃4​Δ​t~π​mp.similar-to-or-equalsdelimited-⟨⟩Δsubscript𝑚𝑝2𝜋𝒩similar-to-or-equals4Δ~𝑡𝜋subscript𝑚𝑝|\langle\Delta m_{p}\rangle|\simeq\sqrt{(2/\pi)\mathcal{N}}\simeq\sqrt{4\Delta\tilde{t}\over\pi}m_{p}. (8)

Identifying Eq. (2) with (8) under the relation mp=M​Apsubscript𝑚𝑝𝑀subscript𝐴𝑝m_{p}=M\,A_{p}, we find that c≃4​Δ​t~πsimilar-to-or-equals𝑐4Δ~𝑡𝜋c\simeq\sqrt{4\Delta\tilde{t}\over\pi} yielding Δ​t~≃0.01similar-to-or-equalsΔ~𝑡0.01\Delta\tilde{t}\simeq 0.01. We use the rescaled time

τ=t~Δ​t~𝜏~𝑡Δ~𝑡\tau={\tilde{t}\over\Delta\tilde{t}} (9)

such that the increase of τ𝜏\tau by 111 corresponds to one year in reality.

IV.2 Fitting the simulated second moment to data

The comparison of the particle-number distribution pt~​(m)subscript𝑝~𝑡𝑚p_{{\tilde{t}}}(m) in the model, varying with the particle density ρ𝜌\rho, and the empirical share distribution may help us to estimate ρ𝜌\rho. We perform simulations of the model with N=508𝑁508N=508 sites and different particle densities set to be integers. Particles are uniformly distributed over all sites initially at t~=0~𝑡0\tilde{t}=0, yielding pt~=0​(m)=δm,ρsubscript𝑝~𝑡0𝑚subscript𝛿𝑚𝜌p_{\tilde{t}=0}(m)=\delta_{m,\rho}. As the stochastic transfers of particles are repeated, the particle-number distribution gets broadened as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The second moment ⟨A2⟩=M−2​⟨m2⟩=M−2​∑mm2​pτ​(m)delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝐴2superscript𝑀2delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝑚2superscript𝑀2subscript𝑚superscript𝑚2subscript𝑝𝜏𝑚\langle A^{2}\rangle=M^{-2}\langle m^{2}\rangle=M^{-2}\sum_{m}m^{2}p_{\tau}(m) increases with the rescaled time τ𝜏\tau and saturates in the late-time regime as shown in Fig. 3 (b). The empirical value of ⟨A2⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝐴2\langle A^{2}\rangle increases steadily from 2×10−52superscript1052\times 10^{-5} to 4×10−54superscript1054\times 10^{-5} for the period 1962-2000 except for the jumps related to oil crises in the middle of the period, which disappear if crude oil (SITC 3330) is excluded [Fig. 1 (a)]. Interestingly, the simulated values of ⟨A2⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝐴2\langle A^{2}\rangle overlap with the empirical values at 90≤τ≤13090𝜏13090\leq\tau\leq 130 as long as ρ≳10greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜌10\rho\gtrsim 10 [Fig. 3 (b)]. This time interval Δ​τ≃40similar-to-or-equalsΔ𝜏40\Delta\tau\simeq 40 is in quite good agreement with the real time interval, 38 years from 1962 to 2000. For ρ≲10less-than-or-similar-to𝜌10\rho\lesssim 10, the overlap period varies with ρ𝜌\rho or does not even exist e.g., for ρ=2𝜌2\rho=2 or 333. Based on these results, we estimate the particle density to be ρ≳10greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜌10\rho\gtrsim 10.

IV.3 Share distribution and condensation

For all ρ≳10greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜌10\rho\gtrsim 10, the simulated share distributions, Pτ​(A)=pτ​(m=A​M)​Msubscript𝑃𝜏𝐴subscript𝑝𝜏𝑚𝐴𝑀𝑀P_{\tau}(A)=p_{\tau}(m=A\,M)M in the period 90≤τ≤13090𝜏13090\leq\tau\leq 130 match excellently the empirical share distributions as shown in Fig. 3 (c). The simulated Pτ​(A)subscript𝑃𝜏𝐴P_{\tau}(A) with ρ=2𝜌2\rho=2 decays faster, failing to fit into the empirical distributions even in the long-time limit.

Given such excellent agreement between the empirical data and the simulation results in the period 90≤τ≤13090𝜏13090\leq\tau\leq 130, one may wonder what will happen in the late-time regime in the simulations. It can hint at the future of international trade. While the broadening of the simulated distribution Pτ​(A)subscript𝑃𝜏𝐴P_{\tau}(A) in the early-time regime does not depend on the particle density, Pτ​(A)subscript𝑃𝜏𝐴P_{\tau}(A) in the late-time regime varies significantly with ρ𝜌\rho. The analytic solution for p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) in the stationary state, derived in Appendix A, shows that similarly to b>2𝑏2b>2, a critical density ρc∼log⁡Nsimilar-tosubscript𝜌𝑐𝑁\rho_{c}\sim\log N exists for b=2𝑏2b=2 and N𝑁N large but finite such that ZN−1,M−m/ZN,Msubscript𝑍𝑁1𝑀𝑚subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀Z_{N-1,M-m}/Z_{N,M} in Eq. (6) behaves as a function of m𝑚m differently depending on whether ρ𝜌\rho is larger than ρcsubscript𝜌𝑐\rho_{c} or not. Consequently, for ρ≲ρcless-than-or-similar-to𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐\rho\lesssim\rho_{c}, p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) has an exponential-decay factor

p∞​(m)≃1ζ​(2)​m2​e−μ​msimilar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑝𝑚1𝜁2superscript𝑚2superscript𝑒𝜇𝑚p_{\infty}(m)\simeq{1\over\zeta(2)m^{2}}e^{-\mu\,m} (10)

with μ=e−ζ​(2)​ρ>0𝜇superscript𝑒𝜁2𝜌0\mu=e^{-\zeta(2)\rho}>0. In contrast, for ρ≫ρcmuch-greater-than𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐\rho\gg\rho_{c}, it has a bump, in addition to the power-law decay form small m𝑚m, as

p∞​(m=M−Nζ​(2)​log⁡2​Nζ​(2)+Nζ​(2)​y)≃ζ​(2)N2​12​π​e​e−14​y2similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑝𝑚𝑀𝑁𝜁22𝑁𝜁2𝑁𝜁2𝑦𝜁2superscript𝑁212𝜋𝑒superscript𝑒14superscript𝑦2p_{\infty}\left(m=M-{N\over\zeta(2)}\log{2N\over\zeta(2)}+{N\over\zeta(2)}y\right)\simeq{\zeta(2)\over N^{2}}{1\over 2\sqrt{\pi e}}e^{-{1\over 4}y^{2}} (11)

for y𝑦y finite, indicating the emergence of a pseudo-condensate Evans et al. (2006): Most particles gather at a single site. Given N=508𝑁508N=508, the estimated particle density ρ≳10greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜌10\rho\gtrsim 10 is above the critical density ρc≃3.5similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝜌𝑐3.5\rho_{c}\simeq 3.5 [Appendix  A] suggesting that the kinetics of product share will enter the pseudo-condensate phase in the long-time limit: The share distribution is expected to get broadened and end up with a power-law decay plus a bump near A=1𝐴1A=1 as an example in Fig. 3 (a) for ρ=50𝜌50\rho=50.

The time scale for such condensation phenomena can be predicted by our simulation results. ⟨A2⟩delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝐴2\langle A^{2}\rangle saturates around τ≃103similar-to-or-equals𝜏superscript103\tau\simeq 10^{3} for ρ≳10greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜌10\rho\gtrsim 10 [Fig. 3 (b)], which suggest that the stationary-state distribution displaying a bump will appear in 103superscript10310^{3} years.

The predictions of the capital condensation in the product space and its time scale demonstrate that our model can be a framework for analyzing the evolution of the share distribution at present and in the future. Its limitations is, however, worthy to note. Most of all, we considered a fixed number, 508, of products traded consistently over the studied period 1962-2000. But in reality, new and old products may enter and leave the space of products, which can make big effects. For instance, the critical density ρcsubscript𝜌𝑐\rho_{c} will be larger with larger N𝑁N, possibly occurring by the rise of new industries. It might pull the international trade out of the pseudo-condensate phase. Also, annual gains Δ​AΔ𝐴\Delta A show large fluctuations for products having small share A𝐴A. Allowing larger chance of gain to small-share, probably new, products might help prevent the condensation of share onto top popular products.

V Probabilistic prediction for individual products

Our model can make a probabilistic prediction also for individual products’ share. We investigate how well the model can predict the growth rates of share {rp​(t)=log10⁡[Ap​(t+1)/Ap​(t)]|1962≤t<2000}conditional-setsubscript𝑟𝑝𝑡subscript10subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡1subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡1962𝑡2000\{r_{p}(t)=\log_{10}[A_{p}(t+1)/A_{p}(t)]|1962\leq t<2000\} for each product p𝑝p. We choose rp​(t)subscript𝑟𝑝𝑡r_{p}(t)’s rather than the share Ap​(t)subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡A_{p}(t)’s since an exceptional variation of the growth rate at a certain time t1subscript𝑡1t_{1} may cause a cascade of shifts in the share Ap​(t)subscript𝐴𝑝𝑡A_{p}(t) for t≥t1𝑡subscript𝑡1t\geq t_{1} like the upper shift of share for oil in Fig. 1 (b). Running simulations with the empirical values at t=1962𝑡1962t=1962, {mp=M×Ap​(1962)}subscript𝑚𝑝𝑀subscript𝐴𝑝1962\{m_{p}=M\times A_{p}(1962)\}, as the initial configuration at τ=0𝜏0\tau=0, we obtain simulated growth rates {r~p(i)​(τ)|i=1,2,…,Ns}conditional-setsuperscriptsubscript~𝑟𝑝𝑖𝜏𝑖12…subscript𝑁s\{\tilde{r}_{p}^{(i)}(\tau)|i=1,2,\ldots,N_{\rm s}\} for all products p𝑝p over the rescaled time period 0≤τ<T=380𝜏𝑇380\leq\tau<T=38 with τ=t−1962𝜏𝑡1962\tau=t-1962 and Nssubscript𝑁sN_{\rm s} the number of simulation runs.

From the rank of the empirical growth rate rp​(t)subscript𝑟𝑝𝑡r_{p}(t) among its Nssubscript𝑁sN_{\rm s} simulated values r~p​(τ=t−1962)subscript~𝑟𝑝𝜏𝑡1962\tilde{r}_{p}(\tau=t-1962)’s, we compute the excess growth rate Rp​(t)subscript𝑅𝑝𝑡R_{p}(t)

Rp​(t)=rank​of​rp​(t)Ns+2−12,subscript𝑅𝑝𝑡rankofsubscript𝑟𝑝𝑡subscript𝑁s212R_{p}(t)={{\rm rank}\ {\rm of}\ r_{p}(t)\over N_{\rm s}+2}-{1\over 2}, (12)

which represents how much the share of product p𝑝p grows faster (slower) than the median of the simulated growth rates. The T𝑇T excess growth rates {Rp​(t)|1962≤t≤2000}conditional-setsubscript𝑅𝑝𝑡1962𝑡2000\{R_{p}(t)|1962\leq t\leq 2000\} for a product p𝑝p will be uniformly distributed between −1/212-1/2 and 1/2121/2 if simulations yield a good probabilistic prediction for the empirical growth rates rp​(t)subscript𝑟𝑝𝑡r_{p}(t)’s of the product p𝑝p Marhuenda et al. (2005); it should not be possible to discern empirical values from the simulated ones. Let us sort t𝑡t’s such that Rp​(t1)≤Rp​(t2)≤⋯≤Rp​(tT)subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡1subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡2⋯subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡𝑇R_{p}(t_{1})\leq R_{p}(t_{2})\leq\cdots\leq R_{p}(t_{T}). If Rp​(t)subscript𝑅𝑝𝑡R_{p}(t)’s are uniformly distributed between −1/212-1/2 and 1/2121/2, then we expect that Rp​(ti)subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡𝑖R_{p}(t_{i})’s will increase linearly with i𝑖i such that Rp​(ti)=R¯i=i/(T+1)−1/2subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡𝑖subscript¯𝑅𝑖𝑖𝑇112R_{p}(t_{i})=\bar{R}_{i}=i/(T+1)-1/2. Deviations from this expectation can quantify the non-uniformity of Rp​(t)subscript𝑅𝑝𝑡R_{p}(t)’s and the failure of the model to predict the growth rates rp​(t)subscript𝑟𝑝𝑡r_{p}(t)’ of product p𝑝p Marhuenda et al. (2005). Therefore we define the unpredictability Upsubscript𝑈𝑝U_{p} of product p𝑝p by

Up=T−1​∑i=1T|Rp​(ti)−R¯i|,subscript𝑈𝑝superscript𝑇1superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑇subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡𝑖subscript¯𝑅𝑖U_{p}=T^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^{T}|R_{p}(t_{i})-\bar{R}_{i}|, (13)

where the tisubscript𝑡𝑖t_{i}’s are sorted such that Rp​(ti)≤Rp​(tj)subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡𝑖subscript𝑅𝑝subscript𝑡𝑗R_{p}(t_{i})\leq R_{p}(t_{j}) for all i<j𝑖𝑗i<j and R¯i=iT+1−12subscript¯𝑅𝑖𝑖𝑇112\bar{R}_{i}=\frac{i}{T+1}-\frac{1}{2}. Upsubscript𝑈𝑝U_{p} of T=38𝑇38T=38 numbers from the uniform distribution may be larger than 0.0940.0940.094 with probability 0.050.050.05. Therefore we consider products with Up>Ucrit=0.094subscript𝑈𝑝subscript𝑈crit0.094U_{p}>U_{\rm crit}=0.094 as unpredictable Marhuenda et al. (2005).

We find that 284 products (56%percent5656\%) are predictable while the remaining 224 (44%percent4444\%) are unpredictable by the model simulations with ρ=400𝜌400\rho=400. The number of predictable products was smaller for other selected values of ρ𝜌\rho. More products are classified as predictable if the model prediction is made for a shorter period than T=38𝑇38T=38 years. Most of the unpredictable products display large positive or negative excess growth rate (Fig. 4 (a)). Interestingly, such deviations are correlated with the nature of the products, as identified in the SITC framework: Growth rates for raw materials and agricultural commodities tend to be smaller than the predicted growth rates, showing the mean excess growth rate ⟨Rp⟩=T−1​∑tRp​(t)delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑅𝑝superscript𝑇1subscript𝑡subscript𝑅𝑝𝑡\langle R_{p}\rangle=T^{-1}\sum_{t}R_{p}(t) negative, while manufactured and especially high-end products such as medical appliances have ⟨Rp⟩delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑅𝑝\langle R_{p}\rangle positive. Figure 4 (b) shows the mean excess growth rates of 66 product categories based on the first two digits of SITC. Chemicals (SITC codes beginning with 5), manufactured goods (6 and 8) and machinery (7) have grown faster than the model prediction while the growth of the share of crude materials (2) was slower than the prediction. The fraction of unpredictable products is larger among manufactured goods than among raw materials (Fig. 4 (c)).

Some products show small |Rp|subscript𝑅𝑝|R_{p}| but large Upsubscript𝑈𝑝U_{p} owing to large fluctuations σp2=T−1​∑t(Rp​(t)−⟨Rp⟩)2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑝2superscript𝑇1subscript𝑡superscriptsubscript𝑅𝑝𝑡delimited-⟨⟩subscript𝑅𝑝2\sigma_{p}^{2}=T^{-1}\sum_{t}(R_{p}(t)-\langle R_{p}\rangle)^{2}; the offer and demand for such products tends to be highly variable or historically determined e.g. railways, warships, and uranium. Thus, our predictability metric captures the fact that some products are more (or less) variable than expected, even if their trend is predicted.

VI Summary and discussion

In this paper we presented a stochastic particle model for the kinetics of products’ shares in international trade, in which sites represent product categories and particles represent a unit of share. From the empirical scaling behavior of the annual variations of product share, the probability of a site to send a particle to another site is set to be proportional to the square of the number of particles at the two sites. Products’ shares are related to the capital invested in those product categories, and therefore such biquadratic transfer rate illuminates a fundamental nature of capital in its activity and interaction.

If the preference to popular products is weaker than that represented by the biquadratic rate, the stationary state will lie in the fluid phase, having no condensation as long as the particle density is finite. Therefore capital in international trade may be considered as being at the edge of the fluid phase. Comparing with the empirical data, we found that the period 1962-2000 covered in the dataset corresponds to a transient period in the model. We were able to determine the time scale and the range of the particle density with which the model predictions match the empirical annual variation and distribution of product share. The estimated particle density turns out to be larger than the critical density, suggesting that a large fraction of trade volumes will concentrate on few product categories in the future. The model simulations show how the share distribution will change with time and predict the time scale of such condensation by using the estimated parameters. The model also makes baseline predictions for the evolution of various industrial sectors, and by comparison, allows us to find other factors such as intrinsic economic fitness Safarzyńska and van den Bergh (2010) at work in addition to the endogenous dynamics represented by our model.

While our model can serve as a simple framework for analyzing the kinetics of capital in the space of products, it could be extended by allowing for the birth and death of product categories, leading to expansion or contraction of the product space. Also intrinsic fitness of products can be considered. Another natural development would be to assume that capital transfers occur on a network of products linked through heterogeneously weighted development pathways Hidalgo et al. (2007); Hidalgo and Hausmann (2009); Hausmann and Hidalgo (2011).

Acknowledgements.
This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grants funded by the Korean Government (No. 2013R1A1A201068845 and No. 2016R1A2B4013204).

Appendix A Derivation of the single-site distribution in the stationary state of the urn model with b=2𝑏2b=2

In Eq. (5), the factorized form of the configuration probability p∞​({m})=ZN,M−1​∏if2​(mi)subscript𝑝𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑁𝑀1subscriptproduct𝑖subscript𝑓2subscript𝑚𝑖p_{\infty}(\{m\})=Z_{N,M}^{-1}\prod_{i}f_{2}(m_{i}) with f2​(m)=m−2/ζ​(2)subscript𝑓2𝑚superscript𝑚2𝜁2f_{2}(m)=m^{-2}/\zeta(2) leads us to find that the grand partition function is given by 𝒵N​(z)=∑M=0∞zM​ZN,M=F2​(z)Nsubscript𝒵𝑁𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑀0superscript𝑧𝑀subscript𝑍𝑁𝑀subscript𝐹2superscript𝑧𝑁\mathcal{Z}_{N}(z)=\sum_{M=0}^{\infty}z^{M}Z_{N,M}=F_{2}(z)^{N} with F2​(z)subscript𝐹2𝑧F_{2}(z) the generating function of f2​(m)subscript𝑓2𝑚f_{2}(m) represented as F2​(z)≡∑m=1∞zm​f2​(m)=∑m=1∞zmζ​(2)​m2=Li2​(z)ζ​(2)subscript𝐹2𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑚1superscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑓2𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑚1superscript𝑧𝑚𝜁2superscript𝑚2subscriptLi2𝑧𝜁2F_{2}(z)\equiv\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}z^{m}f_{2}(m)=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}{z^{m}\over\zeta(2)m^{2}}={{\rm Li}_{2}(z)\over\zeta(2)}, where Lib​(z)subscriptLi𝑏𝑧{\rm Li}_{b}(z) is the poly-logarithm function Lib​(z)=∑m=1∞zm​m−bsubscriptLi𝑏𝑧superscriptsubscript𝑚1superscript𝑧𝑚superscript𝑚𝑏{\rm Li}_{b}(z)=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}z^{m}m^{-b}. This series converges for |z|≤1𝑧1|z|\leq 1 and expanded around z=1𝑧1z=1 as Robinson (1951)

F​(z=e−α)=1+α​log⁡α−αζ​(2)+∑n=2∞(−1)nn!​ζ​(2−n)ζ​(2)​αn.𝐹𝑧superscript𝑒𝛼1𝛼𝛼𝛼𝜁2superscriptsubscript𝑛2superscript1𝑛𝑛𝜁2𝑛𝜁2superscript𝛼𝑛F(z=e^{-\alpha})=1+{\alpha\log\alpha-\alpha\over\zeta(2)}+\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}{(-1)^{n}\over n!}{\zeta(2-n)\over\zeta(2)}\alpha^{n}. (14)

The partition function ZN,Msubscript𝑍𝑁𝑀Z_{N,M} can be recovered from the grand partition function by ZN,M=∮d​z2​π​i​z−M−1​F​(z)Nsubscript𝑍𝑁𝑀contour-integral𝑑𝑧2𝜋𝑖superscript𝑧𝑀1𝐹superscript𝑧𝑁Z_{N,M}=\oint{dz\over 2\pi i}z^{-M-1}F(z)^{N}, where the contour is within the radius of convergence, |z|≤1𝑧1|z|\leq 1 Evans and Hanney (2005). Using Eq. (14), one finds that the dominant contribution is made around z∗=e−μsubscript𝑧superscript𝑒𝜇z_{*}=e^{-\mu} with

μ=e−ρ​ζ​(2),𝜇superscript𝑒𝜌𝜁2\mu=e^{-\rho\zeta(2)}, (15)

and thus the integral is approximated in the limit μ→0→𝜇0\mu\to 0, N→∞→𝑁N\to\infty, and N​μ𝑁𝜇N\mu finite by employing the steepest descent path z=e−μ​y𝑧superscript𝑒𝜇𝑦z=e^{-\mu y} with y𝑦y running from 1−i​∞1𝑖1-i\infty to 1+i​∞1𝑖1+i\infty as

ZN,M=μ​ϕ​(N​μζ​(2)),ϕ​(η)=∫1−i​∞1+i​∞d​y2​π​i​eη​(y​log⁡y−y).formulae-sequencesubscript𝑍𝑁𝑀𝜇italic-ϕ𝑁𝜇𝜁2italic-ϕ𝜂superscriptsubscript1𝑖1𝑖𝑑𝑦2𝜋𝑖superscript𝑒𝜂𝑦𝑦𝑦Z_{N,M}=\mu\,\phi\left({N\mu\over\zeta(2)}\right),\ \phi(\eta)=\int_{1-i\infty}^{1+i\infty}{dy\over 2\pi i}e^{\eta(y\log y-y)}. (16)

Using Eq. (16) into Eq. (6), we find that

p∞​(m)=1ζ​(2)​m2​emN​ζ​(2)​ϕ​(N​μζ​(2)​emN​ζ​(2))ϕ​(N​μζ​(2)).subscript𝑝𝑚1𝜁2superscript𝑚2superscript𝑒𝑚𝑁𝜁2italic-ϕ𝑁𝜇𝜁2superscript𝑒𝑚𝑁𝜁2italic-ϕ𝑁𝜇𝜁2p_{\infty}(m)={1\over\zeta(2)m^{2}}e^{{m\over N}\zeta(2)}{\phi\left({N\mu\over\zeta(2)}e^{{m\over N}\zeta(2)}\right)\over\phi\left({N\mu\over\zeta(2)}\right)}. (17)

For ϕ​(η)italic-ϕ𝜂\phi(\eta) defined in Eq. (16), when η𝜂\eta is large, the contribution near y=0𝑦0y=0 to ϕ​(η)italic-ϕ𝜂\phi(\eta) is dominant, allowing us to approximate it as

ϕ​(η)≃∫−∞∞d​β2​π​e−η​(1+β22)≃12​π​η​e−η.similar-to-or-equalsitalic-ϕ𝜂superscriptsubscript𝑑𝛽2𝜋superscript𝑒𝜂1superscript𝛽22similar-to-or-equals12𝜋𝜂superscript𝑒𝜂\phi(\eta)\simeq\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}{d\beta\over 2\pi}e^{-{\eta}(1+{\beta^{2}\over 2})}\simeq\sqrt{1\over 2\pi\eta}e^{-{\eta}}. (18)

According to our numerical computation, this approximation is good even for η≃1similar-to-or-equals𝜂1\eta\simeq 1. For η≪1much-less-than𝜂1\eta\ll 1, we consider the path surrounding the branch cut of log⁡y𝑦\log y, y=x±i​ϵ𝑦plus-or-minus𝑥𝑖italic-ϵy=x\pm i\epsilon with x∈(−∞,0)𝑥0x\in(-\infty,0) and ϵ→0→italic-ϵ0\epsilon\to 0 such that ϕ​(η)=∫−∞0d​x2​π​i​e−η​(x​log⁡(x−i​ϵ)−x)+∫0−∞d​x2​π​i​e−η​(x​log⁡(x+i​ϵ)−x)=∫0∞d​xπ​eη​(x−x​log⁡x)​sin⁡(η​π​x)italic-ϕ𝜂superscriptsubscript0𝑑𝑥2𝜋𝑖superscript𝑒𝜂𝑥𝑥𝑖italic-ϵ𝑥superscriptsubscript0𝑑𝑥2𝜋𝑖superscript𝑒𝜂𝑥𝑥𝑖italic-ϵ𝑥superscriptsubscript0𝑑𝑥𝜋superscript𝑒𝜂𝑥𝑥𝑥𝜂𝜋𝑥\phi(\eta)=\int_{-\infty}^{0}{dx\over 2\pi i}e^{-\eta(x\log(x-i\epsilon)-x)}+\int_{0}^{-\infty}{dx\over 2\pi i}e^{-\eta(x\log(x+i\epsilon)-x)}=\int_{0}^{\infty}{dx\over\pi}e^{\eta(x-x\log x)}\sin(\eta\pi x), which leads us to

ϕ​(η)italic-ϕ𝜂\displaystyle\phi(\eta) =∫0∞d​qη​π​e(q−q​log⁡q+q​log⁡η)​sin⁡(π​q)absentsuperscriptsubscript0𝑑𝑞𝜂𝜋superscript𝑒𝑞𝑞𝑞𝑞𝜂𝜋𝑞\displaystyle=\int_{0}^{\infty}{dq\over\eta\pi}e^{(q-q\log q+q\log\eta)}\sin(\pi q)
≃∫0∞d​qη​q​eq​log⁡η≃1η​|log⁡η|2.similar-to-or-equalsabsentsuperscriptsubscript0𝑑𝑞𝜂𝑞superscript𝑒𝑞𝜂similar-to-or-equals1𝜂superscript𝜂2\displaystyle\simeq\int_{0}^{\infty}{dq\over\eta}q\,e^{q\log\eta}\simeq{1\over\eta|\log\eta|^{2}}. (19)

Different behaviors of ϕ​(η)italic-ϕ𝜂\phi(\eta) for η≳1greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜂1\eta\gtrsim 1 and η≪1much-less-than𝜂1\eta\ll 1 in Eqs. (18) and (19) give rise to different behaviors of p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) depending on ρ𝜌\rho and N𝑁N as shown in Fig. 5 (a). In case N​μ/ζ​(2)≳1greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑁𝜇𝜁21N\mu/\zeta(2)\gtrsim 1, corresponding to the low-density regime ρ≲ρcless-than-or-similar-to𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐\rho\lesssim\rho_{c} with the critical density defined by

ρc≡1ζ​(2)​log⁡Nζ​(2),subscript𝜌𝑐1𝜁2𝑁𝜁2\rho_{c}\equiv{1\over\zeta(2)}\log{N\over\zeta(2)}, (20)

both ϕitalic-ϕ\phi functions in Eq. (17) behave as Eq. (18). Therefore the particle-number distribution behaves as

p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚\displaystyle p_{\infty}(m) ≃1ζ​(2)​m2​e−N​μζ​(2)​(emN​ζ​(2)−1),similar-to-or-equalsabsent1𝜁2superscript𝑚2superscript𝑒𝑁𝜇𝜁2superscript𝑒𝑚𝑁𝜁21\displaystyle\simeq{1\over\zeta(2)m^{2}}e^{-{N\mu\over\zeta(2)}(e^{{m\over N}\zeta(2)}-1)}, (21)

which is confirmed by the simulation results for various N𝑁N’s and ρ=2𝜌2\rho=2 [Fig. 5 (b)]. One finds that in the regime x=Nζ​(2)​m≪1𝑥𝑁𝜁2𝑚much-less-than1x={N\over\zeta(2)}m\ll 1, p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) has the exponential-decaying term as in Eq. (10).

If N​μ/ζ​(2)≪1much-less-than𝑁𝜇𝜁21N\mu/\zeta(2)\ll 1 or ρ≫ρcmuch-greater-than𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐\rho\gg\rho_{c}, the ϕitalic-ϕ\phi function in the denominator in Eq. (17) behaves as Eq. (19). In the regime ζ​(2)N​[m−(M−Mc)]≪−1much-less-than𝜁2𝑁delimited-[]𝑚𝑀subscript𝑀𝑐1{\zeta(2)\over N}[m-(M-M_{c})]\ll-1 with Mc=N​ρc=[N/ζ​(2)]​log⁡[N/ζ​(2)]subscript𝑀𝑐𝑁subscript𝜌𝑐delimited-[]𝑁𝜁2𝑁𝜁2M_{c}=N\rho_{c}=[N/\zeta(2)]\log[N/\zeta(2)], the ϕitalic-ϕ\phi function in the numerator in Eq. (17) behaves also as Eq. (19) and we find that the ratio of the two ϕitalic-ϕ\phi functions is close to 111, leading to p∞​(m)≃1ζ​(2)​m2similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑝𝑚1𝜁2superscript𝑚2p_{\infty}(m)\simeq{1\over\zeta(2)m^{2}}. In the large-m𝑚m region where ζ​(2)N​[m−(M−Mc)]≳1greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜁2𝑁delimited-[]𝑚𝑀subscript𝑀𝑐1{\zeta(2)\over N}[m-(M-M_{c})]\gtrsim 1, the ϕitalic-ϕ\phi function in the numerator in Eq. (17) behaves as Eq. (18), leading to

p∞​(m)=1ζ​(2)​m2​[log⁡(ζ​(2)N​μ)]2​12​π​ex2−ex,subscript𝑝𝑚1𝜁2superscript𝑚2superscriptdelimited-[]𝜁2𝑁𝜇212𝜋superscript𝑒𝑥2superscript𝑒𝑥p_{\infty}(m)={1\over\zeta(2)m^{2}}\left[\log\left({\zeta(2)\over N\mu}\right)\right]^{2}{1\over\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{{x\over 2}-e^{x}}, (22)

with x=ζ​(2)N​[m−(M−Mc)]=ζ​(2)​(mN−ρ+ρc)=ζ​(2)​mN−log⁡(ζ​(2)N​μ)𝑥𝜁2𝑁delimited-[]𝑚𝑀subscript𝑀𝑐𝜁2𝑚𝑁𝜌subscript𝜌𝑐𝜁2𝑚𝑁𝜁2𝑁𝜇x={\zeta(2)\over N}[m-(M-M_{c})]=\zeta(2)({m\over N}-\rho+\rho_{c})=\zeta(2){m\over N}-\log\left({\zeta(2)\over N\mu}\right). The function ex/2−exsuperscript𝑒𝑥2superscript𝑒𝑥e^{x/2-e^{x}} takes a bell-shape around x0=−log⁡2subscript𝑥02x_{0}=-\log 2 such that it takes a form 12​e​e−14​(x−x0)212𝑒superscript𝑒14superscript𝑥subscript𝑥02{1\over\sqrt{2e}}e^{-{1\over 4}(x-x_{0})^{2}} around x0subscript𝑥0x_{0} which leads to Eq. (11). The scaled plots of p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) in the regime where x𝑥x is finite in Fig. 5 (c) confirm Eq. (22).

Using Eqs. (17), (21) and (22), we can represent p∞​(m)subscript𝑝𝑚p_{\infty}(m) as

p∞​(m)=1ζ​(2)​m2​Ψ​(ζ​(2)​mN;ζ​(2)​(ρc−ρ)),subscript𝑝𝑚1𝜁2superscript𝑚2Ψ𝜁2𝑚𝑁𝜁2subscript𝜌𝑐𝜌p_{\infty}(m)={1\over\zeta(2)\,m^{2}}\Psi\left(\zeta(2){m\over N};\zeta(2)(\rho_{c}-\rho)\right), (23)

where the behaviors of Ψ​(θ;λ)≡eθ​ϕ​(eθ+λ)ϕ​(eλ)Ψ𝜃𝜆superscript𝑒𝜃italic-ϕsuperscript𝑒𝜃𝜆italic-ϕsuperscript𝑒𝜆\Psi(\theta;\lambda)\equiv e^{\theta}{\phi\left(e^{\theta+\lambda}\right)\over\phi\left(e^{\lambda}\right)} can be summarized as

Ψ​(θ;λ)≃{e−eλ​(eθ−1)for​λ≳1,1for​λ≪−1,θ+λ≪−1,λ22​π​e12​(θ+λ)−eθ+λfor​λ≪−1,θ+λ≳1.similar-to-or-equalsΨ𝜃𝜆casessuperscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝜆superscript𝑒𝜃1greater-than-or-equivalent-tofor𝜆11formulae-sequencemuch-less-thanfor𝜆1much-less-than𝜃𝜆1superscript𝜆22𝜋superscript𝑒12𝜃𝜆superscript𝑒𝜃𝜆formulae-sequencemuch-less-thanfor𝜆1greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝜃𝜆1\Psi(\theta;\lambda)\simeq\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}e^{-e^{\lambda}(e^{\theta}-1)}&{\rm for}\,\lambda\gtrsim 1,\\ 1&{\rm for}\,\lambda\ll-1,\theta+\lambda\ll-1,\\ {\lambda^{2}\over\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{{1\over 2}(\theta+\lambda)-e^{\theta+\lambda}}&{\rm for}\,\lambda\ll-1,\theta+\lambda\gtrsim 1.\end{array}\right. (24)

The critical density ρcsubscript𝜌𝑐\rho_{c} is about 3.53.53.5 for N=508𝑁508N=508 according to Eq. (20).
